data.content:
### Upsert Data and Return Values with Supabase (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/docs/ref/javascript/v1/upgrade-guide.mdx

This snippet demonstrates how to perform an upsert operation (insert or update) in a Supabase table. The 'Before' example shows a basic upsert, while the 'After' example uses `.select()` to return the affected row(s) immediately after the upsert.

```ts
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('my_table')
  .upsert({ new_data })
```

```ts
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('my_table')
  .upsert({ new_data })
  .select()
```

--------------------------------

### Filter Supabase Realtime INSERT changes by specific ID

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/content/guides/realtime/postgres-changes.mdx

This snippet demonstrates how to subscribe to `postgres_changes` for `INSERT` events on the `todos` table, specifically filtering for rows where the `id` column equals `1`. It utilizes the `filter` parameter to ensure only relevant insert payloads are received by the client.

```javascript
const changes = supabase
  .channel('table-filter-changes')
  .on(
    'postgres_changes',
    {
      event: 'INSERT',
      schema: 'public',
      table: 'todos',
      filter: 'id=eq.1',
    },
    (payload) => console.log(payload)
  )
  .subscribe()
```

```dart
  supabase
      .channel('table-filter-changes')
      .onPostgresChanges(
          event: PostgresChangeEvent.insert,
          schema: 'public',
          table: 'todos',
          filter: PostgresChangeFilter(
            type: PostgresChangeFilterType.eq,
            column: 'id',
            value: 1,
          ),
          callback: (payload) => print(payload))
      .subscribe();
```

```swift
let myChannel = await supabase.channel("db-changes")

let changes = await myChannel.postgresChange(
  InsertAction.self,
  schema: "public",
  table: "todos",
  filter: .eq("id", value: 1)
)

await myChannel.subscribe()

for await change in changes {
  print(change.record)
}
```

```kotlin
val myChannel = supabase.channel("db-changes")

val changes = myChannel.postgresChangeFlow<PostgresAction.Insert>(schema = "public") {
    table = "todos"
    filter = "id=eq.1"
}

changes
    .onEach {
        println(it.record)
    }
    .launchIn(yourCoroutineScope)

myChannel.subscribe()
```

```python
changes = supabase.channel('db-changes').on_postgres_changes(
  "INSERT",
  schema="public",
  table="todos",
  filter="id=eq.1",
  callback=lambda payload: print(payload)
)
.subscribe()
```

--------------------------------

### Stream INSERT events with Supabase JavaScript client

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/content/guides/realtime/subscribing-to-database-changes.mdx

Listen to all new row insertions in the public schema using the INSERT event. This creates a real-time channel subscription that logs each new row payload to the console. Requires a valid Supabase project URL and API key for authentication.

```javascript
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'
const supabase = createClient('your_project_url', 'your_supabase_api_key')

const channel = supabase
  .channel('schema-db-changes')
  .on(
    'postgres_changes',
    {
      event: 'INSERT',
      schema: 'public'
    },
    (payload) => console.log(payload)
  )
  .subscribe()
```

--------------------------------

### Insert Data and Return Records Explicitly (v2)

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2022-08-16-supabase-js-v2.mdx

In `supabase-js v2`, data manipulation methods like `insert()`, `update()`, and `upsert()` now require an explicit call to `.select()` to return the modified data. This change promotes the 'principle of least surprise' regarding Row Level Security policies, as `data` will be an empty object if `.select()` is omitted.

```ts
// v2
const { data } = await supabase.from('messages').insert({ id: 1, message: 'Hello world' }).select() // select is now explicitly required
```

--------------------------------

### Insert Data into PostgreSQL Table with Serial ID

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2022-09-08-choosing-a-postgres-primary-key.mdx

This SQL command inserts a new row into the `users_serial` table. Since the `id` column is defined as `serial`, it automatically generates a unique ID, and therefore, the `id` column is omitted from the `INSERT` statement.

```sql
insert into users_serial
  (email, name)
values
  ('user@example.com', 'new user');
```

--------------------------------

### Correct RLS Policy for Single INSERT Operation SQL

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/examples/prompts/database-rls-policies.md

This snippet demonstrates the correct way to create an RLS policy for a single `INSERT` operation. Each operation (insert, select, update, delete) requires its own separate policy.

```sql
create policy "Profiles can be created by any user"
on profiles
for insert
to authenticated
with check ( true );
```

--------------------------------

### SQL RLS WITH CHECK Policy for INSERT Operations

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/content/troubleshooting/rls-simplified-BJTcS8.mdx

This example defines an RLS policy for `INSERT` operations using the `WITH CHECK` clause. It ensures that any new row inserted into the `posts` table by an authenticated user has a `user_id` that matches their authenticated UID, preventing users from creating posts under a different user's ID. It includes examples of both failing and successful inserts based on this policy.

```sql
create policy "Allow user to add posts"
on "public"."posts"
as PERMISSIVE
for INSERT
to authenticated
with check(
  (select auth.uid()) = user_id
);
```

```sql
-- Example: failing insert
INSERT INTO posts
VALUES (<false id>, <comment>);

-- Example: successful insert
INSERT INTO posts
VALUES (<real id>, <comment>);
```

--------------------------------

### Test PostgreSQL Conditional Exclusion Constraint with Inserts

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2024-07-11-range-columns.mdx

These SQL statements illustrate the behavior of a conditional `EXCLUDE` constraint based on `table_id` and `duration`. The first `INSERT` reserves a table, the second `INSERT` for the same table with an overlapping duration fails, while the third `INSERT` for a different table with an overlapping duration succeeds, demonstrating table-specific non-overlap.

```SQL
-- Add a first reservation
insert into reservations (title, table_id, duration)
values ('Tyler Dinner', 1, '[2024-07-04 18:00, 2024-07-04 21:00)');

-- Insert fails, because table 1 is taken from 18:00 - 21:00
insert into reservations (title, table_id, duration)
values ('Thor Dinner', 1, '[2024-07-04 20:00, 2024-07-04 22:00)');

-- Insert succeeds because table 2 is not taken by anyone
insert into reservations (title, table_id, duration)
values ('Thor Dinner', 2, '[2024-07-04 20:00, 2024-07-04 22:00)');
```

--------------------------------

### Bulk Insert JSON with Default Values using Supabase Client

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2023-07-12-postgrest-11-1-release.mdx

Demonstrates how to perform a bulk insert of JSON data into a PostgreSQL table using the Supabase client library. It shows how default column values are respected for unspecified fields (e.g., 'baz') and how generated IDs are handled, providing both the SQL table definition and the JavaScript insertion code.

```sql
create table
  foo (
    id bigint generated by default as identity primary key,
    bar text,
    baz int default 100
  );
```

```jsx
const { error } = await supabase
  .from('foo')
  .insert([
	  { "bar": "val1"
	  }
	, { "bar": "val2"
	  , "baz": 15
	  }
	], defaultToNull: false)
  .select()
```

--------------------------------

### Test PostgreSQL Exclusion Constraint with Overlapping Inserts

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2024-07-11-range-columns.mdx

These SQL statements demonstrate the effect of an `EXCLUDE` constraint on `duration` columns. The first `INSERT` adds a reservation, and the second `INSERT` for a different reservation with an overlapping duration fails due to the active constraint.

```SQL
-- Add a first reservation
insert into reservations (title, duration)
values ('Tyler Dinner', '[2024-07-04 18:00, 2024-07-04 21:00)');

-- The following insert fails because the duration overlaps with the above
insert into reservations (title, duration)
values ('Thor Dinner', '[2024-07-04 20:00, 2024-07-04 22:00)');
```

--------------------------------

### Insert Data with PostGIS for Supavisor Performance Testing (SQL)

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2023-08-11-supavisor-1-million.mdx

This SQL INSERT statement is used to simulate a realistic workload for performance testing of Supavisor and PgBouncer. It inserts data into a 'positions' table, including dynamic values for various fields and geographic coordinates utilizing the PostGIS extension, to assess query duration under load.

```sql
insert into positions (
    stud_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    title,
    reports_to,
    timestamp,
    location,
    email
)
values (
    ${name},
    'Virtual ${name}',
    'User ${name}',
    'Load Tester',
    1,
    ${Date.now()},
    st_point(-73.946${x}, 40.807${y}),
    'vu${name}@acme.corp'
);
```

--------------------------------

### Check PostgreSQL auto-increment sequence synchronization

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/content/troubleshooting/inserting-into-sequenceserial-table-causes-duplicate-key-violates-unique-constraint-error-pi6DnC.mdx

This SQL snippet retrieves the maximum value from a sequenced column and the next value from its associated sequence. It helps identify if a PostgreSQL auto-incrementing sequence is out of sync with the actual primary key values in a table. If `nextval` is not greater than the `MAX` value, resynchronization is likely needed.

```sql
SELECT MAX(<sequenced_column>) FROM <table_name>;

SELECT nextval(pg_get_serial_sequence('<public.table_name>', '<sequenced_column_name>'));
```

--------------------------------

### Ingest Movie Documents into Postgres with Slonik Driver

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/www/_blog/2022-10-14-postgres-full-text-search-vs-the-rest.mdx

Inserts movie documents into a PostgreSQL database using the Slonik driver. The function filters out documents with invalid release dates and performs an upsert operation on the movies table. It handles conflict resolution by updating existing records with the same ID.

```javascript
// Ingesting search documents
async function ingest({ document, pool }) {
  // Ignore rows without proper release dates
  if (document.release_date.trim().length === 0) {
    return
  }

  await pool.query(sql`
insert into movies (
	id, 
	title, 
	original_title, 
	overview, 
	released_at
)
values
(
	${document.id},
	${document.title},
	${document.original_title},
	${document.overview},
	${new Date(document.release_date).toISOString()}::timestamptz
)
on conflict (id) do update set
  title=${document.title}, 
	original_title=${document.original_title}, 
	overview=${document.overview}, 
	released_at=${new Date(document.release_date).toISOString()}::timestamptz
;
`)
}
```

--------------------------------

### Reset PostgreSQL auto-increment sequence to maximum value

Source: https://github.com/supabase/supabase/blob/master/apps/docs/content/troubleshooting/inserting-into-sequenceserial-table-causes-duplicate-key-violates-unique-constraint-error-pi6DnC.mdx

This SQL command resets a PostgreSQL auto-incrementing sequence to ensure it continues from a value greater than the current maximum primary key in the table. It's used to resolve 'duplicate key violates unique constraint' errors caused by out-of-sync sequences, typically after data imports or restores. It's recommended to back up the database before running this command.

```sql
SELECT SETVAL('public.<table_name>_<column_nam>_seq', (SELECT MAX(<column_name>) FROM <table_name>)+1);
```
